region_of_origin: Switzerland/France; widely served in Singapore steakhouse chains and Western hawker stalls
food_subgroup: Crumbed and deep-fried or baked stuffed chicken breast; processed meat filling (ham and processed cheese); Singapore restaurant portion ~200g cooked

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Chicken Cordon Bleu as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be a stuffed and crumbed poultry product; no exact FoodOn term available as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken, stuffed and crumbed, fried — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0C0A used as closest parent for crumbed stuffed chicken. No exact FoodEx2 term for Chicken Cordon Bleu with ham and processed cheese filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Cordon Bleu as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore restaurant serving of Chicken Cordon Bleu is approximately 200g (one whole crumbed stuffed chicken breast piece), as served at steakhouse chains such as Jack's Place and Collin's, and at Western hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Cordon Bleu — Component Calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken breast, ham, processed cheese, breadcrumbs)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients: boneless chicken breast (FDC 171477), cured ham, processed American-style cheese, and seasoned breadcrumbs. Weighted proportions reflect a typical Singapore restaurant Cordon Bleu (~200g cooked): ~55% chicken breast, ~20% ham, ~10% processed cheese, ~15% breadcrumb coating. Values represent per-100g of the composite cooked dish. Confidence: medium, as exact restaurant formulations vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Breadcrumb-Coated Chicken Products — Analogous Estimate Based on Seasoned Breadcrumb Coating GI (~70) Moderated by High Protein and Fat Content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Cordon Bleu. GI estimated at ~55 using the University of Sydney GI Database as the reference framework. The breadcrumb coating alone has a GI of approximately 65–70 (white bread analogue). However, the high protein content (~19g/100g) from chicken breast and ham, combined with the fat content (~12.5g/100g) from cheese and frying, substantially attenuates the glycemic response, yielding an estimated composite GI of ~55 (low category). Glycemic load per 200g serving = (55 × 10g carbohydrate per 100g × 2) / 100 = ~11.0 (moderate GL). This estimation is consistent with published literature on protein-fat attenuation of GI in mixed meals.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content: ~1,050mg per 200g serving (525mg/100g), representing 44–53% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000mg, driven primarily by the cured ham filling (~400–500mg per piece) and processed cheese (~200mg per piece)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should treat Chicken Cordon Bleu as a high-sodium dish equivalent to processed snack foods; a single serving consumes more than half the daily sodium budget before any soup, sauce, or side dishes, and substituting with plain grilled chicken breast at the same restaurant reduces sodium by approximately 80%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~11 per 200g serving) due to breadcrumb coating carbohydrates, substantially attenuated by high protein (38g per serving) and fat content which slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Cordon Bleu is more glycaemically compatible for Type 2 Diabetes management than pasta or battered fish, with a GL of ~11 per serving; however, patients should monitor accompanying side dishes (chips, rice, or bread) which are the primary glycaemic risk in a Western restaurant meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8g per 200g serving, 4g/100g) from processed cheese and frying oil, combined with high sodium (~1,050mg per serving) and processed meat (ham, WHO/IARC Group 1 carcinogen at habitual high intake) represent multiple cardiovascular risk factors in a single dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Chicken Cordon Bleu should be an occasional choice only; the combination of processed meat (ham), saturated fat from cheese, and high sodium exceeds recommended limits for a single meal, and a baked rather than deep-fried preparation with reduced-sodium ham would meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed ham is pro-inflammatory via advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation during curing; high sodium from ham and processed cheese may exacerbate fluid retention in RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids with oedema should limit Chicken Cordon Bleu due to its high sodium content from processed ham and cheese; the chicken breast component itself is anti-inflammatory neutral, and substituting with a plain grilled chicken breast eliminates both the processed meat and excess sodium concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Cordon Bleu (Singapore Restaurant Style) is a classic French-inspired Western dish widely served at Singapore steakhouse chains such as Jack's Place and Collin's, and at Western hawker stalls. It consists of a boneless chicken breast that is butterflied, stuffed with cured ham and processed cheese (typically American-style processed cheese slices), rolled, coated in seasoned breadcrumbs, and either deep-fried or baked until golden. The Singapore restaurant portion is approximately 200g cooked weight per piece. The dish delivers approximately 510 kcal, 38g protein, 25g fat (saturated fat ~8g), and 20g carbohydrate per serving. The most clinically significant feature is its very high sodium content of approximately 1,050mg per 200g piece — equivalent to 44–53% of the WHO daily sodium limit — sourced primarily from the cured ham filling (~400–500mg) and processed cheese (~200mg), not from the chicken or breadcrumb coating. This makes it a hidden sodium risk in what appears to be a straightforward protein dish. The GI is estimated at ~55 (low category) with a glycemic load of ~11 per serving, as the high protein and fat content substantially attenuate the glycaemic impact of the breadcrumb coating. Texture is crispy on the outside from the crumb coating, with a moist, tender chicken interior and a melted cheese-ham filling. Nutritional strengths include high protein (38g per serving) supporting satiety and muscle maintenance. Key concerns are high sodium (hypertension risk), saturated fat from cheese and frying, and the presence of processed meat (ham, WHO/IARC Group 1 carcinogen at habitual high intake). Source: component calculation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: is chicken cordon bleu high in sodium

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients (chicken breast, cured ham, processed cheese, breadcrumbs) weighted to reflect a typical Singapore restaurant portion. No direct laboratory analysis of Singapore restaurant Chicken Cordon Bleu exists in approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate using the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Exact restaurant formulations vary across Jack's Place, Collin's, and hawker stalls, introducing moderate uncertainty in sodium and fat values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Chicken Cordon Bleu dish (chicken breast + ham + processed cheese + breadcrumbs) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; component-level fatty acid data would require weighted calculation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite cooked dish not reported in the component calculation source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the primary chicken breast component; trace amounts from processed cheese are below detection threshold and reported as 0). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — chicken, ham, processed cheese, and breadcrumbs contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). Halal status marked false as standard ham is pork-derived; halal-certified versions using chicken ham exist at some Singapore establishments but cannot be assumed. Singapore HPB database does not contain a direct entry for Chicken Cordon Bleu; USDA FoodData Central component calculation used as the closest approved-source equivalent. GI estimated at ~55 using University of Sydney GI Database analogous framework; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Cordon Bleu found in approved GI sources.
